description: Fix bugs, resource leaks, dead code, and missing guards discovered during backend audit of the MCP server package.

userStories.title: Fix cleanupStaleAdapterFiles unreachable rejection branch and inaccurate delete count
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the stale adapter file cleanup to accurately report deletion results so that log messages reflect actual outcomes, not always-success counts.
acceptanceCriteria: Promise.allSettled in cleanupStaleAdapterFiles wraps unlink calls WITHOUT an inner .catch(() => {}), so rejected results are properly surfaced
The deleted count in the log message reflects only actually successful deletions, not the total number of stale files
Same fix applied to the Promise.allSettled in writeAdapterFile (line 74) — remove inner .catch(() => {})
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files.ts

Bug: Lines 74 and 132 wrap unlink() calls with .catch(() => {}) INSIDE Promise.allSettled(). Since .catch() converts rejections to fulfilled promises, the allSettled results always have status 'fulfilled' — the 'rejected' branch on line 135-138 is dead code. The `deleted` counter always equals `staleFiles.length` regardless of actual deletion success.

Fix: Remove the inner .catch(() => {}) from both locations. Let unlink rejections surface naturally through Promise.allSettled, so the rejection branch works correctly and the count is accurate.

Line 74: `await Promise.allSettled(oldFiles.map(f => unlink(join(adaptersDir, f)).catch(() => {})));`
→ `await Promise.allSettled(oldFiles.map(f => unlink(join(adaptersDir, f))));`

Line 132: `const results = await Promise.allSettled(staleFiles.map(f => unlink(join(adaptersDir, f)).catch(() => {})));`
→ `const results = await Promise.allSettled(staleFiles.map(f => unlink(join(adaptersDir, f))));`

Also line 219 in cleanupStaleExecFiles has the same pattern — fix there too, and add a count of successful deletions to the log message (currently always logs the total).

userStories.title: Fix extension_force_reconnect tool always timing out
userStories.description: As a user, I want the extension_force_reconnect browser tool to return success immediately so that it does not always produce a misleading 30-second timeout error.
acceptanceCriteria: extension_force_reconnect uses fire-and-forget dispatch (sends the JSON-RPC message directly via state.extensionWs) instead of dispatchToExtension which expects a response
The tool returns a success message immediately after sending the disconnect signal, similar to how reload-extension.ts works
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/extension-force-reconnect.ts

Bug: Line 19 uses `dispatchToExtension(state, 'extension.forceReconnect', {})` which creates a pending dispatch with a 30s timeout and waits for a response. But force-reconnect kills the WebSocket connection, so the response never arrives — the tool ALWAYS returns a timeout error after 30 seconds, even on success.

Fix: Follow the pattern used by reload-extension.ts (platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/reload-extension.ts lines 20-33), which sends the message directly via state.extensionWs.send() and returns immediately with a success message. Do NOT use dispatchToExtension.

The handler should:
1. Check state.extensionWs is connected
2. Send the JSON-RPC notification directly: `state.extensionWs.send(JSON.stringify({ jsonrpc: '2.0', method: 'extension.forceReconnect', id: getNextRequestId() }))`
3. Return a success message like 'Force reconnect signal sent — the extension will disconnect and reconnect.'
4. Catch WebSocket send errors and return an appropriate error message

Import getNextRequestId from '../state.js' and sendToExtension from '../extension-handlers.js' if needed.

userStories.title: Fix logger duplicating error message in debug stack trace output
userStories.description: As a developer, I want debug log output to show the error message exactly once so that stack traces are clean and readable.
acceptanceCriteria: When logging at debug level with an Error argument, the error message appears exactly once (not duplicated before the stack trace)
The stack trace (which already includes the message as its first line in V8) is used directly without prepending last.message
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/logger.ts

Bug: Line 47: `return [...rest, last.message + '\n  ' + last.stack];`

In V8 (Node.js), Error.stack already includes the error message as the first line (format: `ErrorType: message\n  at ...`). Prepending `last.message + '\n  '` before `last.stack` produces output like:
  `Some error\n  Error: Some error\n  at ...`

Fix: Change line 47 to use just the stack:
  `return [...rest, last.stack];`

This produces clean output:
  `Error: Some error\n  at ...`

userStories.title: Fix removeLocalPlugin error being swallowed from caller
userStories.description: As a developer, I want removeLocalPlugin to correctly propagate errors to callers so that removePlugin reports the real failure instead of a misleading 'not found in localPlugins' error.
acceptanceCriteria: When the async IIFE inside removeLocalPlugin throws (e.g., atomicWrite fails), the error is propagated to the caller (removePlugin)
The configWriteMutex is correctly reset to a resolved promise on failure so subsequent writes are not blocked
The `removed` variable is correctly communicated across the async boundary

userStories.title: Add timeout to spawnSync calls in version-check.ts
userStories.description: As a server operator, I want npm version check commands to have a timeout so that a hanging npm process does not block the entire MCP server indefinitely.
acceptanceCriteria: spawnSync in fetchLatestVersion has a timeout option (e.g., 10 seconds)
If the timeout is exceeded, fetchLatestVersion returns null (same as other failure modes)
The timeout is logged at debug level when it fires
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/version-check.ts

Bug: Line 27: `spawnSync('npm', ['view', packageName, 'version'], { stdio: ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe'] })` has no timeout. If npm hangs (DNS resolution, registry auth prompt, network issue), the entire Node.js event loop blocks indefinitely. This is called during reload for each npm plugin.

Fix: Add `timeout: 10_000` to the spawnSync options:
`spawnSync('npm', ['view', packageName, 'version'], { stdio: ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe'], timeout: 10_000 })`

When timeout fires, spawnSync returns with `raw.signal === 'SIGTERM'` and `raw.status === null`. The existing check `raw.error || raw.status !== 0` will catch this (status is null which is not 0).

Also apply the same fix to searchNpmPlugins in plugin-management.ts line 153:
`spawnSync(platformExec('npm'), ['search', searchTerm, '--json'], { stdio: ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe'], timeout: 15_000 })`

Use 15s for search since npm search is inherently slower than npm view.

Also fix getGlobalNodeModulesPaths in resolver.ts — find the spawnSync('npm', ['root', '-g'], ...) call and add timeout: 10_000.

userStories.title: Fix mtime polling timer ID race between generations
userStories.description: As a developer running in dev mode, I want mtime polling self-cleanup to never accidentally cancel the new generation's timer so that file change detection remains reliable across hot reloads.
acceptanceCriteria: The old generation's interval callback does NOT call clearInterval on state.fileWatching.mtimePollTimerId (which may belong to the new generation)
Instead, the old interval only returns early without clearing any state — stopFileWatching already handles cleanup before the new generation starts
